results.question: What caps work with the 30ml Boston Round?
results.response:
The 30ml Boston Round has a 20-400 neck finish, and it's compatible with three types of closures.

For a simple cap, there's a black lid in the 20-400 thread — clean and straightforward. If you're using a roller ball plug, there are six tall cap options in a range of finishes: matte gold, matte silver, matte black, shiny gold, shiny silver, and shiny black. And if you're going the dropper route, there are six dropper assemblies available — black or white bulb, with plain, gold-trimmed, or silver-trimmed collar caps.

Are you pairing these with a specific formula — an oil, a serum, or something else? That'll help me point you to the right closure style.

results.question: I need a dropper for my 9ml Cylinder. What fits?
results.response:
Good news — I have a clear picture for you. One important thing to flag first: the 9ml Cylinder actually comes in two neck sizes depending on the variant. The fine mist sprayer versions use a 13-415 neck, and the roller ball versions use a 17-415 neck.

Unfortunately, neither neck size shows a dedicated dropper in the compatible components list — the 13-415 neck supports sprayers and caps, and the 17-415 neck supports sprayers, roll-on caps, and lotion pumps.

Before I point you in another direction, could you tell me a little more about what you're filling the bottle with? That would help me suggest the best dispensing solution — whether that's a different bottle that does accept a dropper, or an alternative applicator that would work just as well for your formula.

What finish direction are you working with for the brand?

results.question: I have a thick perfume oil, like honey consistency. What roll-on bottles do you recommend?
results.response:
I want to make sure you get the best result here — a honey-thick oil will actually cause problems with a roll-on. The roller ball relies on the liquid flowing freely around it to dispense, and with a thick, viscous oil it simply won't spin properly. You'd end up with a frustrating experience for your customers.

For a honey-consistency perfume oil, the ideal applicators are:

A tola bottle — this is the traditional format for attars and thick perfume oils. You apply by dabbing directly, which is exactly how thick oils are meant to be used. It's elegant, functional, and deeply rooted in fragrance tradition.

A dropper bottle — a calibrated glass dropper gives you precise, controlled dispensing without any mechanical parts that can clog. Great for both retail and gifting formats.

Shall I pull up our tola and dropper options so you can see what's available? And do you have a size or aesthetic in mind — something compact and portable, or a more substantial presentation piece?

results.question: I'm making an eau de parfum. Should I use roll-on bottles?
results.response:
For an eau de parfum, roll-on bottles aren't the right fit. EDPs are alcohol-based and quite thin, and that liquid will seep past a roller ball and leak — roll-ons are really designed for oils and serums that have enough viscosity to sit at the opening without running through.

For an EDP, a fine mist sprayer is the classic choice — it delivers that signature soft cloud of fragrance and feels premium in the hand. That's what most fine fragrance brands use, and it's what we'd recommend here.

Are you thinking of a particular bottle size or style for the line?

results.question: I'm packaging a thick body lotion. Would a dropper bottle work?
results.response:
For a thick body lotion, a dropper wouldn't be the right fit — the viscosity is too heavy for a dropper to dispense cleanly, and it can clog or leave residue in the tube.

What you'd want is a lotion pump. It's engineered specifically for thick emulsions, giving your customers a smooth, controlled dose every time — which also elevates the perceived quality of the product.

Would you like me to pull up our lotion pump bottle options, or do you have a size and style in mind already?

results.question: Tell me about your Boston Round collection.
results.response:
The Boston Round is a timeless workhorse — that classic shoulder-and-body silhouette that has been a staple of fragrance, apothecary, and wellness packaging for generations. We carry it in three sizes — 15ml, 30ml, and 60ml — across three glass colours: clear, amber, and cobalt blue, with amber being especially popular for essential oils and light-sensitive formulations. The neck finishes are 18-400 on the smaller sizes and 20-400 on the 30ml and 60ml, both of which are widely compatible with sprayers, droppers, caps, and reducers.

results.question: I only need 3 bottles. Can I order that?
results.response:
Absolutely — there's no unit minimum here, just a $50 order floor. So three bottles is perfectly fine as long as the order totals $50 or more.

Depending on the style you choose, three bottles may or may not reach that threshold on their own. If they come in under, the easiest move is to add matching closures, a second bottle style to sample, or a few extra pieces — you'd end up with a proper test kit rather than just the bottles.

What style are you looking at?

results.question: Do you manufacture your own glass?
results.response:
We don't manufacture glass ourselves — Best Bottles is a sourcing and supply partner. We work with a curated network of artisan glassmakers worldwide to bring premium vessels to your brand.

What sets us apart is that we use these same bottles for Nemat's own fragrance lines, sold at Sephora, Ulta, and Whole Foods — so every piece in our collection has been validated at the retail level before it ever reaches your hands.

Are you working on a specific project I can help you find the right vessel for?

results.question: Why should I choose Best Bottles over other suppliers?
results.response:
There are a few things that genuinely set us apart, and I'll be straight with you about what they are.

First, practitioner credibility. We use these exact bottles for Nemat International's own fragrance lines — sold at Sephora, Ulta, and Whole Foods. When we recommend a vessel, it's not theoretical. We've already put our own brand name on it at retail.

Second, the system guarantee. Every bottle and component we sell is sourced as a matched set. Caps, sprayers, droppers, roll-ons — all tested for compatibility with the bottles they ship alongside. Mismatched thread sizes are the single most common reason a packaging order becomes a crisis. We've eliminated that problem at the source.

Third, we occupy a very specific sweet spot in the market. If you're past the Etsy-and-farmers-market stage but not yet running container-load volume through a Berlin Packaging account, the enterprise suppliers won't give you real attention — and the DIY suppliers won't give you real quality. We work with brands in that scaling gap every day, and our minimums and service model are built around it.

Fourth, expertise without pretension. Our team understands fragrance, essential oils, cosmetics, and the formulation considerations that affect packaging choices — viscosity, UV sensitivity, alcohol compatibility, label bleed, component fitment. You're not talking to a warehouse. You're talking to people who think about this the way you do.

No unit minimums, domestic warehousing in Union City, CA, and a $50 order floor that makes small-batch testing genuinely accessible.

What are you working on? I'd love to help you find the right vessel for it.

results.question: I'm launching a luxury perfume line. Should I go with Elegant or Diva bottles?
results.response:
Here's how I'd frame the distinction:

The Elegant is the more classic silhouette — tall, slender, refined. It comes in four sizes (15ml through 100ml), in both clear and frosted glass, and offers a range of neck finishes, which gives you flexibility as your line grows. It's the kind of bottle that reads immediately as fine fragrance on a vanity or retail shelf.

The Diva is more sculptural and statement-making — a broader, more dramatic form that commands attention. It comes in three sizes (30ml, 46ml, and 100ml), also in clear and frosted, but with a single neck finish across all sizes, which actually simplifies component sourcing. The 46ml is a distinctive size you don't see everywhere, which can be a real differentiator on shelf.

Both come in the full range of cap finishes — gold, silver, black — and accept fine mist sprayers, which is what you'd want for an eau de parfum.

What's the visual direction of your brand — more understated and editorial, or bold and collectible?

results.question: I need bottles for my essential oil business. Where do I start?
results.response:
Essential oils are a wonderful place to begin — the packaging choices here matter a great deal, both for product integrity and brand presentation.

The two most important questions before we look at anything specific:

First, are your oils light-sensitive? Most essential oils degrade with UV exposure, so amber glass is the standard choice for preservation. Clear or frosted glass works beautifully for display, but amber is the functional workhorse.

Second, how do you want your customers to apply the oil — with a dropper for precise dispensing, a roll-on for direct skin application, or an open pour with a reducer cap?

Once I know those two things, I can point you to exactly the right vessels and components. What are you working with?

results.question: What does 18-415 mean? I see it on your product pages.
results.response:
Great question — that notation is the industry-standard neck finish code for glass and plastic bottles.

The first number, 18, is the outer diameter of the bottle's neck in millimetres. The second number, 415, refers to the thread pattern — specifically the number of turns and the pitch of the threads that a closure screws onto.

So an 18-415 neck is 18mm wide with a 415-style thread. Any cap, sprayer, dropper, or pump labelled 18-415 is engineered to mate with that exact finish — and that's the guarantee behind every component we sell alongside those bottles.

Is there a specific bottle or closure you're trying to match up?
